title: The Almanack of MM — Mental Model Quiz: Philosophy and Bonus
source: The Almanack of MM by Eric Jorgenson (2020)

questions.question: MM gives three answers to 'what is the meaning of life.' What is the third answer, and why does he consider it both meaningful and unsatisfying?
questions.short_answer: The third answer is thermodynamic: living systems locally reverse entropy while globally accelerating the Universe toward heat death — a state of perfect uniformity where everything is 'one.' It's meaningful as a physical theory but unsatisfying because it isn't a purpose you chose.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM draws on the second law of thermodynamics to argue that life might have a cosmic function: concentrated systems (humans, civilizations, computers) temporarily reverse entropy locally, but in doing so they accelerate the Universe's overall march toward maximum entropy — the heat death where all energy is uniformly distributed and nothing is distinguishable from anything else. In that terminal state, everything is effectively 'one thing.' The more complex and creative the system you build — a family, a company, a civilization — the more you push toward that convergence. MM finds the theory intellectually compelling and almost poetic, but admits it is 'not a very satisfying purpose' because it is a description imposed by physics, not a meaning you freely create. It illustrates his broader point that any externally supplied meaning ultimately invites the next 'why,' making it an infinite regress of turtles all the way down.

questions.question: Why does MM argue that the 'important part' of the meaning-of-life question is the question itself, not any answer?
questions.short_answer: Because any answer given to you by someone else will feel like secondhand noise; the only meaning that sticks is one you excavate for yourself through sustained inquiry. The process of digging is what transforms you.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's first answer to the meaning of life is explicitly personal: wisdom borrowed from Buddha or anyone else will 'sound like nonsense' until you arrive at it through your own experience. This is not intellectual modesty — it is an epistemological claim that meaning cannot be transferred; it can only be discovered. The question itself is a kind of tool for self-excavation. Sitting with it for years or decades forces you to clarify your actual values, strip away inherited assumptions, and build a foundation that is genuinely yours. When you do arrive at an answer you're satisfied with, MM says, it becomes 'fundamental to your life' in a way that no borrowed creed ever could. This is consistent with his broader philosophy: personal truth must be verified through first-person experience, not accepted on authority.

questions.question: MM dismisses afterlife narratives and draws a specific analogy to make his point. What is that analogy, and what philosophical conclusion does it support?
questions.short_answer: He compares the state after death to the state before birth — a condition of perfect indifference to everything, including yourself and all people you love. This supports his view that death is a return to non-experience, not a transition to judgment or reward.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's argument is both empirical and rhetorical: before you were born, you had zero concern for anyone or anything — your loved ones, humanity's fate, AI, Mars. That state of total non-caring was not painful; it simply was nothing. After death, he argues, it will be exactly the same. He finds it intellectually incoherent that a rational God would judge an eternal soul based on seventy years of behavior in one physical life. The analogy to pre-birth also dissolves the fear of death: there was no suffering before you existed, so there is no reason to expect suffering after you stop existing. This positions death not as an ending to dread but as a return to the neutral baseline from which you briefly departed. It is a stoic, materialist reframing that strips death of its terror without requiring any supernatural consolation.

questions.question: MM says dishonesty — even social white-lying — creates a specific cognitive cost he is unwilling to pay. What is that cost, and why does he treat it as a core reason to avoid dishonesty entirely?
questions.short_answer: Dishonesty splits your mind into multiple concurrent threads: one tracking what you actually think, another tracking what you said, and a third planning future cover. This fragments presence and turns every conversation into a chess game.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's objection to dishonesty is not primarily moral but psychological and computational. When you say something other than what you believe, you create a divergence between your internal state and your expressed state. Maintaining that divergence requires ongoing mental overhead — you must remember what you told to whom, anticipate follow-up questions, and manage future interactions to stay consistent with past fabrications. MM describes this as running 'multiple threads' in his mind simultaneously, which destroys the quality of present-moment attention. His solution is absolute: he simply refuses to spend time around anyone with whom he cannot be completely honest, treating honesty as an environment requirement rather than a virtue he exercises with difficulty. He also notes the deeper implication — 'before you can lie to another, you must first lie to yourself' — pointing out that self-deception precedes and enables external deception, making the stakes even higher.

questions.question: MM says 'all benefits in life come from compound interest' and applies this beyond finance. How does this principle shape who he chooses to work with and spend time around?
questions.short_answer: He only wants relationships and collaborations that he intends to sustain for life, because compounding only works over long time horizons. Short-term thinking in any counterparty is a disqualifying signal he acts on immediately.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's compound-interest mental model generalizes from money to every domain: relationships, health, habits, knowledge, and love all grow nonlinearly over time — but only if the relationship is long-term. A short-term thinker, even in a single transaction with someone else, reveals a fundamental orientation that is incompatible with compounding. MM says if he sees someone treating a third party with short-term logic, he stops wanting to work with that person at all — not because of personal offense, but because their time discount rate is too high to produce the compounding dynamics he values. This is also why he only pursues work with 'long-term payout': he is essentially asking which investments will still be growing in twenty years. The same logic extends to his insistence on peer relationships — hierarchical power dynamics tend to be transactional and fragile, while peer relationships built on mutual respect are the kind that compound across decades.

questions.question: MM quotes Charlie Munger: 'To find a worthy mate, be worthy of a worthy mate.' How does this principle differ from conventional romantic advice, and what does it reveal about MM's view of self-development?
questions.short_answer: Conventional advice focuses on finding the right person; Munger's inversion focuses on becoming the right person. MM treats self-development not as self-help but as the prerequisite for accessing the relationships and outcomes you want.
questions.elaborated_answer: Most romantic (and career) advice is outward-looking: strategies for identifying, attracting, or persuading others. Munger's inversion flips the causal arrow — the bottleneck is not finding worthy people but deserving them. MM says he is 'lucky' he had developed his values by the time he met his wife, but then immediately corrects himself: without those values, he wouldn't have deserved her. This is not false modesty; it is a claim about selection dynamics. People with strong values can detect alignment or misalignment quickly and will only commit to those who match them. If you have not done the internal work, you are invisible or unattractive to the very people you most want in your life. The implication is that the path to great external outcomes runs entirely through internal development — a counterintuitive reversal of most goal-setting frameworks.

questions.question: What makes MM's 'Rational Buddhism' different from traditional Buddhism, and what is his epistemic criterion for accepting or rejecting any belief?
questions.short_answer: Rational Buddhism strips Buddhism of anything not personally verifiable or scientifically reconcilable — past lives, chakras, and supernatural powers are discarded; meditation, present-moment awareness, and the monkey mind are kept because MM has verified them firsthand.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's operating procedure is to treat his own first-person experience and scientific evidence as the only valid authorities. He asks of every Buddhist claim: can I verify this myself, or can it be confirmed through science? For claims that pass — meditation improves mental clarity, there is a base layer of awareness beneath anxious thought — he adopts them. For claims that fail — karma from past lives, chakras opening, levitating from meditation — he rejects them, not because they are certainly false, but because they are not falsifiable and he cannot confirm them personally. Crucially, he does not dismiss Buddhism wholesale; he considers it 'the oldest, most time-tested spiritual philosophy regarding the internal state of each of us' and places it on one pole of his worldview, with evolutionary biology on the other. The two poles are, he argues, fully reconcilable. His test — 'try everything, test it for yourself, be skeptical, keep what's useful, discard what's not' — is essentially the scientific method applied to inner life.

questions.question: MM defines wisdom as 'understanding the long-term consequences of your actions.' Why is this definition unusual, and what does it imply about how most people make decisions?
questions.short_answer: Most people define wisdom as knowledge or good judgment; MM reframes it as a temporal skill — the ability to trace causality far into the future. This implies that most poor decisions are not made from ignorance of facts but from failure to extend the time horizon of analysis.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's definition is deceptively precise. It does not mention knowledge, virtue, or experience — the conventional ingredients of wisdom. Instead, it isolates time as the critical variable. A decision that seems correct in the short term can be catastrophically wrong when you extend the consequence chain by five or ten steps. Conversely, actions that feel costly or uncomfortable now may compound into enormous long-term benefits. This connects directly to his rule about only working with long-term thinkers and his compound-interest philosophy: if wisdom is about long-term consequences, then short-term thinkers are by definition unwise, regardless of their intelligence or intentions. It also explains his aphorism 'if wisdom could be imparted through words alone, we'd all be done here' — truly understanding long-term consequences requires living through them, not just being told about them. Wisdom is experiential precisely because the future is too complex to simulate from a verbal description.

questions.question: MM says everyone starts innocent, gets corrupted, and wisdom is the 'return to virtue by way of knowledge.' What does the phrase 'by way of knowledge' add that makes this different from simple repentance or self-discipline?
questions.short_answer: Repentance or discipline suppresses vice through willpower; wisdom eliminates vice by genuinely understanding why it is harmful. The 'by way of knowledge' phrase means the vices lose their appeal once you see their full consequences clearly.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's formulation is subtle: the path back to virtue runs through knowledge, not through force of will. This is a fundamentally different mechanism. Self-discipline fights desire head-on, which is exhausting and often fails. But if you truly understand — not just intellectually acknowledge — the long-term damage of anger, dishonesty, or short-term thinking, the desire for those behaviors weakens naturally. You don't need to white-knuckle away from anger; you just see it as 'a hot coal you hold in your hand while waiting to throw it at somebody,' and the motivation to hold the coal collapses. This is why MM says he 'no longer believes in anger' rather than 'I try to suppress anger.' Knowledge transforms the valuation function, not just the behavior. It also explains why he treats corruption as universal — everyone accumulates bad mental models during development — and why the journey is lifelong rather than a single conversion event.

questions.principle: The Present Is All We Have
questions.question: MM says 'all death really means is that there are no more future moments.' How does this reframe death, and what does it imply about the nature of the past?
questions.short_answer: By defining death as the cessation of future moments rather than the loss of a self, MM strips it of existential dread. It also implies the past is functionally dead already — a 'fictional memory tape' with no more reality than a dream.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's formulation decouples death from identity and centers it on time. Death is not the destruction of a soul or person; it is simply the point at which no new moments are added to a sequence. This has two radical implications. First, it means every prior moment has already 'died' in the same sense — once a moment passes, it is gone as completely as if it never occurred. The past is a reconstruction, a 'fictional little memory tape,' not a real place that persists. Second, it means the only thing that is actually alive — in the sense of being real and available — is this moment. MM says he barely remembers what he said two minutes ago, which is not a limitation but a feature: it demonstrates how completely each moment is its own complete unit. This connects to his aphorism 'you're dying and being reborn at every moment' — the self is not a continuous thread but a series of present-moment instances, and remembering or forgetting that is a choice.
questions.section: Philosophy — The Present Is All We Have

questions.principle: The Present Is All We Have
questions.question: MM says 'inspiration is perishable — act on it immediately.' What mental model underlies this warning, and how does it connect to his broader philosophy of the present moment?
questions.short_answer: Inspiration is a present-moment phenomenon: it arises in specific conditions that will not repeat in the same way. Waiting to act on it is functionally the same as discarding it, because the future self who acts will be operating without the original motivational context.
questions.elaborated_answer: The perishability of inspiration is a direct corollary of MM's presentism. If only this moment is real, then the energy, clarity, and motivation that arise right now are also moment-specific. They are not stored anywhere and cannot be reliably retrieved on demand. Future-you will face different circumstances, different moods, different competing priorities — and the creative or productive spark that existed in this moment will be diluted or gone entirely. This is why MM frames it as a warning about action, not a piece of motivational rhetoric. Delaying action on inspiration is not neutral; it is a decision to let a perishable asset spoil. The same logic appears in his approach to reading, relationships, and decisions — when something is clear and the moment is right, the cost of waiting is higher than it appears, because you are not simply deferring a choice but forfeiting the specific conditions that made the choice obvious.
questions.section: Philosophy — The Present Is All We Have

questions.principle: The Present Is All We Have
questions.question: MM quotes Homer's Iliad: 'Everything is more beautiful because we're doomed.' How does mortality function in MM's philosophy — as a problem to overcome, or as a feature of experience?
questions.short_answer: For MM, mortality is a feature that concentrates beauty and urgency into the present. Impermanence is not a flaw in life's design; it is precisely what makes each moment irreplaceable and therefore precious.
questions.elaborated_answer: This quote from Homer does heavy philosophical work in MM's framework. Most cultures treat mortality as a problem — something to be transcended through religion, legacy, or achievement. MM inverts this: the doomed nature of everything is exactly what makes it beautiful. A sunset is gorgeous partly because it is ending. A relationship is precious partly because it is finite. If moments lasted forever or could be perfectly replicated, they would lose the quality of uniqueness that makes them matter. MM's presentism and his acceptance of death as the cessation of future moments converge here: if the present is all we have and mortality is real, then the rational response is not grief or denial but heightened attention to what is here now. This is the practical dimension of 'be present above all else' from his rules — presence is not just a meditative technique but an acknowledgment that what is happening right now will not happen again in exactly this way.
questions.section: Philosophy — The Present Is All We Have

questions.question: MM says he doesn't read for self-improvement but out of 'curiosity and interest,' and that 'the best book is the one you'll devour.' Why is this a counterintuitive reading philosophy, and what does it imply about how most people approach learning?
questions.short_answer: Most people read for utility — to extract applicable lessons. MM reads for genuine fascination, trusting that deep engagement with interesting ideas will produce better learning than disciplined reading of 'important' books that bore you.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's reading philosophy rejects the self-improvement industry's premise that value comes from choosing the right books and extracting their lessons systematically. He argues instead that the quality of your absorption is determined by your genuine interest, not your willpower or intention. A book you devour — one you stay up to finish, think about unprompted, recommend to everyone — teaches you more than a 'must-read' you slog through dutifully. This is also why he admits he skips two-thirds of his actual reading when listing recommendations: he reads 'junk,' reprehensible content, things he disagrees with, because following his curiosity wherever it leads produces richer intellectual compounding than following a curated syllabus. The deeper mental model is that intrinsic motivation produces exponentially better outcomes than extrinsic motivation — a principle he applies across his whole life, not just reading.

questions.question: MM describes Marcus Aurelius's Meditations as 'absolutely life-changing' and explains why through a specific observation about the book's context. What is that observation, and what principle does it reveal?
questions.short_answer: Aurelius was probably the most powerful person on Earth when he wrote Meditations — yet it is a private diary of someone struggling with the same internal challenges as anyone else. This proves that external success and power do not resolve internal suffering; the inner work is always separate and always required.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's insight about Meditations is not about Stoic philosophy per se — it is about the evidential weight of Aurelius's position. An emperor ruling the known world, with unlimited resources and power, who could have anything he wanted, chose to write a private journal about controlling his own mind and becoming a better person. The diary was never intended for publication; it was written to himself. This makes it a uniquely honest document and a uniquely powerful data point: if ultimate worldly success doesn't end the internal struggle, nothing external will. MM's conclusion — 'right there, you figure out success and power don't improve your internal state — you still have to work on it' — is a direct refutation of the assumption that achievement produces peace. It validates the Buddhist and philosophical traditions he practices, which hold that happiness is an inside job requiring dedicated, lifelong cultivation regardless of external circumstances.

questions.question: MM identifies two poles of his entire worldview: evolution and Buddhism. What does placing them as poles — rather than choosing one — reveal about how he constructs his philosophy?
questions.short_answer: By holding both poles simultaneously, MM refuses to let either science or spirituality be the complete story. Evolution explains the external structure of the human animal; Buddhism explains how to manage the internal experience of being that animal.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's two-pole framework is a deliberate intellectual structure. Evolution — as the binding principle of biology and human behavior — explains why we have egos, why we fear death, why we seek status, and why cooperation and competition coexist. It is descriptive and mechanistic. Buddhism — specifically the non-fanciful, internally verifiable parts — explains how to work with that evolved machinery to produce a calmer, more present, more virtuous inner life. The poles are not in conflict; they operate at different levels of analysis. MM explicitly says he wants to write about how Buddhist tenets can be mapped onto a virtual reality simulation — implying that even the most ancient spiritual insights are compatible with a fully scientific, computational view of reality. This willingness to synthesize rather than choose is itself a meta-mental model: he is looking for the reconciliation point between seemingly opposed frameworks rather than pledging allegiance to one tribe.

questions.question: MM calls Nassim Taleb's Skin in the Game 'one of the best business books I've ever read' but specifically praises that it 'doesn't masquerade as a business book.' What distinction is MM drawing, and why does it matter?
questions.short_answer: A book that labels itself a business book constrains the reader's frame of reference and signals that its insights are domain-specific. Skin in the Game smuggles fundamental principles about risk, accountability, and ethics into an accessible form without the genre's narrowing effect.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's praise here is about the difference between domain knowledge and principles. Most business books offer tactics or case studies: how to negotiate, how to manage people, how to build a brand. Their framing signals 'this applies to business contexts.' Taleb's book is about something more fundamental — that people who bear the consequences of their decisions make better decisions, and that any system where advisors, experts, or managers are insulated from downside produces bad outcomes. This principle applies to business, medicine, politics, philosophy, and personal life equally. By not masquerading as a business book, it doesn't trigger the genre-filtering that causes readers to store lessons as 'business tips' rather than universal principles. MM values books that operate at the level of general mental models rather than domain-specific advice — which is why his reading list blends physics, philosophy, history, evolution, and science fiction: all are sources of foundational models, not just information about specific fields.

questions.question: MM says he 'always spent money on books' and 'never viewed that as an expense — that's an investment.' How does this reflect a specific mental model about the economics of knowledge, and how does it differ from how most people budget for learning?
questions.short_answer: Most people treat books as a discretionary consumer purchase; MM treats them as compounding assets — a book that changes one decision or mental model can generate returns that dwarf its cost by orders of magnitude.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM's investment framing for books is an application of his broader compound-interest philosophy. If reading is the 'ultimate meta-skill that can be traded for anything else' — as his rules state — then a book is not just content but a potential upgrade to the decision-making engine that generates all future outcomes. One insight that changes how you evaluate risk, relationships, or business models could be worth millions of dollars in avoided mistakes or captured opportunities. The asymmetry is extreme: a $20 book that shifts your mental model on leverage or long-term thinking has unlimited upside. This reframing also explains why he reads broadly and without guilt — even 'embarrassing' books, genre fiction, books he disagrees with. The expected value of any book that genuinely engages your curiosity is positive because the knowledge compounds. The calculus flips: not buying a potentially transformative book is the reckless choice, not the prudent one.

questions.question: MM praises Jiddu Krishnamurti as 'extremely influential' and specifically values his instruction to 'look at your own mind at all times.' How does this connect to MM's broader epistemology?
questions.short_answer: Krishnamurti's core instruction is that the observer and the observed are the same — understanding yourself requires watching your mind without the distortion of belief systems or borrowed frameworks. This aligns with MM's insistence on first-person verification over accepted doctrine.
questions.elaborated_answer: Krishnamurti was a radical anti-guru: he famously disbanded the organization built around him, refusing to be anyone's teacher, and spent his life arguing that psychological liberation cannot be transmitted — it can only be self-discovered through direct observation of one's own mental processes. MM describes him as 'uncompromising' and 'very direct,' precisely the qualities he admires in thinkers. The instruction to observe your own mind maps directly onto MM's Rational Buddhism framework: just as he rejects Buddhist claims he cannot personally verify, Krishnamurti rejects all inherited authority — religious, psychological, or philosophical — as an obstacle to direct seeing. Both are pointing at the same thing: that borrowed maps of reality (ideologies, belief systems, identities) prevent you from experiencing reality directly. For MM, Krishnamurti is essentially a philosopher who built an entire career around the same epistemological foundation he applies to everything — verify it yourself or don't accept it.

questions.question: MM says reading enough naturally causes you to 'gravitate toward theory, concepts, nonfiction' — away from narrative and fiction. What does this trajectory reveal about how he thinks the optimal reading diet evolves over a lifetime?
questions.short_answer: As a reader matures, the marginal value of new stories decreases while the marginal value of abstract principles increases — you have enough narrative data to extract the patterns, and what you need now are better frameworks to organize and apply them.
questions.elaborated_answer: MM describes his own reading evolution: comics and sci-fi in childhood, then history and news, then psychology and popular science, then theory and nonfiction. This is not a hierarchy of sophistication — he still reads and recommends science fiction, and great fiction like Borges appears high on his list. The trajectory is about diminishing returns on different types of content. Early in a reading life, stories build world models, populate your imagination with scenarios, and develop pattern recognition. But once you have accumulated enough narrative experience, what accelerates learning most is moving toward the generative level: the theories, models, and principles that explain why the stories turned out the way they did. Nonfiction, physics, philosophy, and evolutionary biology all operate at this higher level of abstraction. You also become, as he says, a 'connoisseur' — your taste sharpens, your filtering tightens, and you naturally seek the books with the highest density of new ideas per page, which is why he singles out Sapiens as 'chock-full per page.'

questions.question: MM's Life Formulas I expresses Happiness = Health + Wealth + Good Relationships and then decomposes each term recursively. What does the recursive, algorithmic structure of this formula reveal about his approach to problem-solving?
questions.short_answer: By expressing life outcomes as recursive algorithms rather than goals, MM treats personal flourishing as an engineering problem: identify the inputs, optimize each layer, and the desired output emerges from the system rather than from willpower applied to a vague aspiration.
questions.elaborated_answer: The formula's structure is revealing: Happiness is not defined as a feeling to pursue but as the output of a system whose components can be measured and optimized independently. Health breaks down into exercise, diet, and sleep; each of those further breaks down into specific practices. Wealth breaks down into income and return on investment; income breaks down into accountability, leverage, and specific knowledge — all terms MM has defined precisely elsewhere. This decomposition has two strategic advantages. First, it makes abstract goals concrete and actionable: instead of 'be healthier,' you have 'do high-intensity resistance training, eat natural foods, sleep 8-9 hours without alarms.' Second, it exposes the real bottlenecks: if your happiness is low, you can audit which layer of the hierarchy is underperforming rather than applying undifferentiated effort. The algorithmic framing also reflects MM's view that success is not magic — it is the predictable output of well-designed systems, and designing those systems is a learnable skill.
